diagnosis.conclusion: The ~14,055% spike in electronics order_count during the week of 2025-12-08 is best explained by a discrete, nationwide pre-Christmas seasonal or promotional demand event targeting electronics specifically. The time series shows a sharp, isolated single-week spike: 54 orders (week of 2025-12-01) → 588 orders (week of 2025-12-08) → 54 orders (week of 2025-12-15), confirming it is a discrete one-off event, not a trend.

All other categories rose only 14–21% in the same week (home_decor +21%, toys +19%, fashion +18%, sports +17%, health_beauty +16%, food_drink +15%), confirming the surge is electronics-specific and not a broad platform event.

All Brazilian states rose in the anomaly week (ES +67×, TO +64×, PA +56×, SP +33×, MG +31×, RJ +29×, etc.), with no single state disproportionately responsible, ruling out a regional campaign or localized fulfillment event.
All payment types rose uniformly in the anomaly week (boleto +33×, credit_card +30×, voucher +29×, debit_card +25×), ruling out a payment-method-specific unlock as the cause.

Sample orders from the anomaly window are all dated 2025-12-08, show normal 'delivered' statuses, and contain high-value electronics line items (R$ 757–R$ 2,042 per order), consistent with genuine consumer purchases rather than data ingestion artifacts.

Week 4 revenue of R$32.4K is actually ~17% above the true pre-spike baseline average of R$27.7K, not below it. diagnosis.conclusion: Starting the week of 2026-02-02, voucher-based payment orders collapsed by ~94% in both order count (from ~40/week to 1–6/week) and payment value (from ~BRL 18,241/week avg to ~BRL 1,160/week avg). All other payment types — credit_card, boleto, and debit_card — held steady or grew over the same period, definitively ruling out a broad data pipeline failure or regional demand collapse. boleto, credit_card, and debit_card payment values were unaffected across the same window (e.g. credit_card ranged BRL 9.7M–15.1M/week throughout), ruling out a broad data pipeline or platform outage.
